coredata.dc:title: A non-linear model predictive control strategy to minimise mechanical degradation effects of lithium-ion battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Samad, Hudair
Malik, M.S.S.
Gulzar, M.
coredata.dc:description: Mechanical stresses are not only developed inside the active material of the composite electrode, but also in the solid electrolyte interface layer (SEI) due to the graphite expansion. Due to diffusion induced stress (DIS) phenomena, stresses in the negative electrode are generated during charging and discharging cycles. This article presents the proposed optimal charging profile explicitly incorporating the effects of mechanical degradation. Non-linear model predictive control approach along with Gauss pseudo-spectral method is used to optimise charging trajectories. It is assumed that active material is not the weakest material but system is modelled based on SEI break and repair effect. The dynamics of battery is represented by a single particle model. The simulated results are compared with benchmark constant current constant voltage (CCCV) strategy. Furthermore, proposed methodology is compared with optimal CCCV using cycle life ageing experimental scenario. It is estimated that stress amplitude decreased by 7% and nominal capacity increased by 11% using the proposed optimal charging profile.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optimal charging
Non-linear model predictive control
Lithium-ion battery
Pseudo-spectral methods

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Comparison proposed strategy with experimental scenario

head.ce:title: A non-linear model predictive control strategy to minimise mechanical degradation effects of lithium-ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering and Materials Science, Queen Mary University of London, E1 4RB, United Kingdom

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Engineering and Materials Science, Queen Mary University of London

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering and Materials Science, Queen Mary University of London, E1 4RB, United Kingdom

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mechanical Engineering Department, University of Engineering & Technology Taxila, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Mechanical Engineering Department, University of Engineering & Technology Taxila

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Mechanical Engineering Department, University of Engineering & Technology Taxila, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mechanical stresses are not only developed inside the active material of the composite electrode, but also in the solid electrolyte interface layer (SEI) due to the graphite expansion. Due to diffusion induced stress (DIS) phenomena, stresses in the negative electrode are generated during charging and discharging cycles. This article presents the proposed optimal charging profile explicitly incorporating the effects of mechanical degradation. Non-linear model predictive control approach along with Gauss pseudo-spectral method is used to optimise charging trajectories. It is assumed that active material is not the weakest material but system is modelled based on SEI break and repair effect. The dynamics of battery is represented by a single particle model. The simulated results are compared with benchmark constant current constant voltage (CCCV) strategy. Furthermore, proposed methodology is compared with optimal CCCV using cycle life ageing experimental scenario. It is estimated that stress amplitude decreased by 7% and nominal capacity increased by 11% using the proposed optimal charging profile.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the continuous environmental legislations, there is an increasing demand of electric vehicles (EVs). It has been resulted of research for improved lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) which are essential part of EVs [1,2]. In this regard, ageing of LiBs under the domain of calendar and cycle life has been investigated by various researchers [3,4]. Battery community accepted the fact that calendar ageing occurs due to side reactions between electrolyte and electrodes [5,6]. This type of ageing can damage the battery vigorously at high temperature and state of charge (SOC) [7,8]. However, dealing within the cycle, ageing aspect is somehow challenging due to the factors including interaction of side reactions with volume expansion/compression and temperature gradients in the cell [9,10]. This article addresses the interaction of such phenomenon during intercalation (charging) of a lithium-ion battery. Moreover, factors such as current rate and depth of discharge (DOD) are also investigated.

The concept of mechanical degradation is a fundamental tool to fully understand the cyclic ageing of LIBs. Diffusion induced stress (DIS) principle is one of the pioneer to give insight of the cyclic ageing [11,12]. It states that maximum stress in electrode particle increases with c-rate and size of particle. Later on, many published articles reported the stress effects due to particle shape [13], phase changes [14] or non-constant young modulus [15]. Authors also combine DIS and fatigue approaches to observe the capacity fade during cycling [16,17]. The DIS model considers only the fracture of the graphite particle neglecting any damage to solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer. However, this idea is later discarded by [18,19]; which reveals that fracture of particles occur only in severe conditions, such as very low temperature. As SEI is a non-homogeneous porous material [20], it is quite easier to undergo fracture than the graphite [21,22]. Effect of SEI on cycle ageing is studied by Purewal et al. [17]. This work considered separate formulation of stresses in active material and SEI layer as explained in [23].

This article explicitly incorporates the mechanical degradation effects in battery charging that minimises the stress amplitude which ultimately act as a source of fracture. Battery damage can be controlled by putting constraints on different variables of batteries, which affect the overall charging process [24,25]. Constant current constant voltage (CCCV) is widely acclaimed charging strategy but it cannot deal with degradation processes satisfactorily [26]. However, model-based algorithms based on electro-chemical battery dynamics can be used to charge the batteries in safer manner [27,28]. Electro-chemical models are always preferred over equivalent circuit models as latter do not cope with the internal variables constraint of the battery [29]. Dynamic programming [30,31], genetic algorithms [32,33] and pseudo-spectral optimisation [34,35] are common algorithms used for optimal charging. Computational burden is one of the main reason of not using it commercially, however some researchers tried to reduce it by proposing efficient algorithm [26]. Direct control of unwanted reactions in battery and adaptation to variations in battery are main advantages of model-based controllers [36]. To the best of our knowledge, no research work till date considers mechanical degradation effects in optimal charging algorithms.

This work can be seen as an attempt to minimise SEI layer effect due to diffusion induced stresses in a lithium-ion battery. Operating battery in healthy regime, reducing the capacity fade and predicting the life of a battery are main parameters, are investigated in this work. To control the degradation effects, proposed online methodology considers single particle model (SPM) to represent dynamics of battery. SPM gives appropriate agreement between veracity and computational performance compared to others reported electrochemical models [37]. Legendre polynomials are used to depict the dynamics of battery. Complexity of reformulated model can be reduced by choosing correct number and type of Legendre polynomials [38]. The resulting non-linear model is challenging because of its non convex behaviour and complexity. Traditional optimisation algorithms can solve these kind of problems but computational cost is observed to be the major barrier. Gauss pseudo-spectral method is used to optimise the charging scenario because of its high convergence rate. Research reported that pseudo-spectral methods can easily solve non-linear and non-convex optimisation problems effectively [39]. Continuous problem can be easily transformed to non-linear programming (NLP) problem, which can be further solved using commercial software.

This paper’s novel and unique contribution is the formulation of a non-linear model predictive control (NMPC) framework which considers the effects of mechanical degradation. A dynamic model predicts the future instances of the controlled plant by optimising the performance cost. Cost of the problem is defined in terms of states and input arrays. Receding horizon approach is used to optimise the control actions in every sampling instance. Physics based constraints handling throughout the charging process is the key element for proposed strategy. Optimisation results of the NMPC framework are compared with traditional CCCV approach.

This work is also compared with optimal CCCV approach considering experimental scenario published in [23]. High energy 18650 lithium-ion battery with nominal capacity of 2.05 has been used. All the cycling tests simulations are recorded at 35 °C. In the experimental work, voltage adjustment is done after 100 cycles while in simulation procedure is adjusted every 10 cycles. The relationship between open circuit voltage and SOC is critical in this optimisations framework for accurate adjustment of voltage levels. Experimental curve is given by Laresgoiti et al. [23], to calculate the overpotential at current state of charge of battery.

The remainder of this article is organised as follows: Section 2 presents the governing equations and reformulated SPM. Theory of mechanical degradation is presented in Section 3 while Gauss pseudo-spectral method is briefly discussed in Section 4 . Section 5 presents the problem formulation while Section 6 explains the mathematical formulation of NMPC strategy. Section 7 presents the results and discussion of the proposed algorithm. Section 8 concludes the article.

The governing equations of single particle model (SPM) are presented in this section. Orthogonal projection techniques are used to convert partial differential equations to ordinary differential equations [40]. To achieve reasonable trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency, a physics based SPM is used in this work [41,42]. The parameters of SPM and reference potential curves for both electrodes are obtained from [43]. Assumptions for modelling SPM can be found in [44].

Fick’s second law of diffusion gives the information about solid phase diffusion dynamics. The governing differential equation is

Partial differential diffusion Eq. (1) needs to be reformulated into ordinary differential equation. Galerkin method with Legendre spatial basis functions is used to discretise Fick’s law. The entire process is briefly described below and further details can be found in [38].

It is assumed that concentration in any electrode is only a function of time and radial periphery. The lithium ions concentration
c
( r , t )
can be approximated by a linear combination of Legendre polynomials and corresponding time variables:

. Outputs of the system can be computed algebraically.

Solid electrolyte interface layer (SEI) is a porous material formed as the result of irreversible dissolution of the electrolyte with active particle of the electrode. Although, the exact configuration of SEI layer is not fully explicable but researchers agree on its non-homogeneous nature [45]. During intercalation and de-intercalation of the lithium ions, active material particle is surrounded by thick layer of SEI due to volume change [19]. Cycle life of lithium-ion battery can be reduced due to continuous expansion and compression of the material. The stresses generated due to charging/discharging is known as diffusion induced stresses, thoroughly explained in literature [11,12]. The stress and strain for an elastic spherical body can be related by following mathematical expressions [46].
(21)
ϵ
r
=
1

3

ϵ
r
,
σ
r
and
ϵ
t
,
σ
t
are the radial and tangential strain, stresses respectively, E is the young modulus; ν is the Poisson’s ratio, c is active material particle concentration and Ω is the partial volume of solute in the solid material. It is assumed that elastic properties do not change with lithium concentration and also no external forces acting on the particle. It means stress tensor can be defined by evaluating following equilibrium equation [47]:

It has been observed that volume expansion due to intercalation do not normally cross the limit of yield point [11,17]. But this does not exclude the possibility of damage to the battery during cycling. Fracture can occur even if the cyclic loads are lower than critical point due to the continuous loading, causing microscopic structural damage to the battery. The Wohler curves [48] are empirical results of certain materials which relate the number of life cycles to the amplitude of the cycle stress. The amplitude of cycle stress is expressed as:
(32)
σ
a m p l
=
σ
m a x
−
σ
m i n
2
To calculate the maximum number of cycles to failure (
N
m a x
), Basquin power law is used.
(33)
N
m a x
=
(
σ
y i e l d
σ
a m p l
)
1
m
where
σ
y i e l d
is the critical stress for static failure and m is the slope of
σ
a m p l
vs. N curve in the bi-logarithmic diagram. Single stress events are considered and assuming no calendar ageing effect, capacity loss is equivalent to the cyclic capacity loss which is proportional to the stress amplitude [23].
(34)
C a
p

m
where
A
I
is a quantification factor of capacity loss. It is worth noting here that if stress amplitude becomes zero, assumption of no-calendar ageing effect does not valid any more.

This section briefly explains the theory of Gauss pseudo-spectral method. Non-linearity, mainly due to constraints and computational cost of online method, makes the optimisation problem difficult. The first requirement in GPM is to change the time interval from arbitrary bounds
t ∈
[
t
0
,
t
f
]
to the interval
τ ∈
[ − 1 , 1 ]
by
(35)

+
t
0
)
2
The collocation points used in GPM are all interior to the interval
[ − 1 , 1 ]
[39]. The initial point
τ
0
= 0
and final point
τ
f
= 1
are also taken in to account. The Lagrange interpolating polynomials at a set of collocation points are building blocks for integration approximation matrix. These polynomials can be expressed as [49].

The above equation is a Gauss quadrature approximation to the continuous definition of final states. Cost function can be discretised using the quadrature rule, written as
(44)

where

and

are

quadrature weight and point (or node) respectively. The cost (41) and non-linear boundary constraints (42) form the NLP which can be further solved by optimisation techniques.

The main aim of this work is to minimise strain energy during charging. For this purpose, an optimal charging problem is used for demonstration of the concept. Stress amplitude should be lowered to reduce the damage to the battery caused by cyclic loading. Optimisation problem can be formulated as:
where q is the control parameter,
e
( r , t )
is the time rate change of strain energy,
S O
C
r e f
is reference state of charge, and
I
m a x
,
V
m a x
are the maximum current and voltage respectively. The goal of this problem is to charge the cell to a desired SOC,
S O
C
r e f
, along with minimising stress amplitude. SOC can be written as (7)
(46)
S O
C
n
( t )
=
ϕ
0
( r )
β
0
( t )
+
ϕ
2
( r )

2
( t )
+
ϕ
4
( r )

4
( t )
+
ϕ
6
( r )
β
6
( t )
c
m a x , n
To compare the results with the proposed optimal control algorithm, this article uses standard CCCV charging method, defined as follows
Results from the proposed strategy are also compared with (47) using experimental procedures conducted in [23]. The published results are the extension of experimental work by same authors on graphite/NMC HE 18650 Sanyo cell [8].

The main idea of model predictive control (MPC) is to optimise system by predicting future input values. This section discusses the formulation and implementation of the NMPC control algorithm based on optimisation problem.

A dynamic model predicts the future responses of controlled plant. System can be represented as in discrete state space representation form as
(48)
x
( k + 1 )
= A
x
( k )
+ B
u
( k )
where
x
( k )
and
u
( k )
are prediction model state and input vectors at
k
t h
sampling instant respectively. A and B are system matrices. The prediction of states is generated by solving the model forward over N sampling intervals (prediction horizon). The state and input sequences can be expressed as
(49)
u
( k )
=
u
( k | k )
u
( k + 1 | k )
u
( k + 2 | k )
.
.
u
( k + N − 1 | k )
,
x
( k )
=
x
( k + 1 | k )
x
( k + 2 | k )
x
( k + 3 | k )
.
.
x
( k + N | k )
where
u
( k + j | k )
and
x
( k + j | k )
denote input and state at time
k + j
, predicted at time k respectively. It means that
x
( k + j | k )
evolves according to prediction model as
(50)
x
( k + j + 1 | k )
= A
x
( k + j
| k )
+ B
u
( k + j |
k )
,
j = 0 , 1 , 2 , … . . .
with initial condition defined as
x
( k | k )
= x
( k )
. In this particular work, there are three states and one input. The final dynamic prediction model in the form of state space is shown below
(51)

is redundant because of zero dynamics but must be known to find state of charge of battery. It can be algebraically calculated using following output equation:

is the Lagrange polynomial.

The future predictions are computed by minimising predicted performance cost, defined in terms of states and inputs sequences. Cost function
J
( k )
as defined in (41), is a function of
u
( k )
and optimal input sequence for the problem denoted as
u
∗
( k )
. It can be written as
(54)
u
∗
( k )
= a r g
m i
n
u
J
( k )
In this work, the cost function as defined in (45), is discretised using Legendre Gauss quadrature rule (44). It can be written as
J =

can be solved using (31). State of charge at Legendre Gauss point is written as

In all of future optimal input sequence
u
∗
( k )
, only the first element is input to the plant:
(57)
u
( k )
=
u
∗
( k | k )
The process of evaluating
u
∗
( k )
and implementing the first element of
u
∗
is then repeated at each sampling instance
k = 0 , 1 , 2 , …
.. Due to this repetition of prediction at every instance, it is known as an online optimisation. The prediction horizon keeps its constant length throughout the optimisation process and therefore approach is known as receding horizon strategy. In this work, the term (
t
f
−
t
0
) denotes the prediction horizon as seen in above discretised cost function (55). Future N states
[
β
0
( t )
,
β
2
( t )
,
β
4
( t )
]
T
and N inputs
I
( t )
are predicted at current sampling instant. In next sampling instant,
t
f
−
t
0
will be same as in last instant but initial values of system is changed.

Apart from any obvious equality constraints that satisfy the dynamics of model, every control problem encounters inequality constraints on input and state variables. As noted from problem (45), one input and two non-linear constraints are part of this optimisation exercise. The linear inequality constraint is of the form
(58)
A
e q
x ≤
b
e q
Using Eq. (39), we get following form of linear inequality constraint
I
N
− T t × A
( 1 , 2 )
×
P
N
− T t × A
( 1 , 3 )
×
P
N
− T t × B
( 1 , 1 )
×
P
N

N

N
− T t × A

×

− T t × A

β

where

N
,

N
are

identity and zero matrices respectively.
1

is all-ones matrix of order
N × 1
.
P
N
is integration approximation matrix of order
N × N
, as defined in (40). A and B are system matrices, taken from (20). The variable
T t
is defined as

. According to Gauss pseudo-spectral notation,
x

means
[

T
. This size of
A
e q
and
b
e q
matrices depends on the chosen prediction horizon. The two non-linear constraints are voltage
V
c e l l
and side reaction overpotential
η
s r
; both are function of states and input. In pseudo-spectral notation, it can be written as
(60)
V
(
τ

)
=
R

T

[
ln
(
J
p

)

)

)

(

+ I

(

=
R

T

(

)

n

τ
i
)
)
Finally, the algebraic cost (55), along with linear (58) and non-linear constraints ((60), (61)) make up the non-linear programming problem. It is further solved by MATLAB function “fmincon” in this work.

Proposed algorithm is shown in Fig. 1. Fresh battery cell undergoes formation cycle and an initial SEI layer formed on the surface of graphite. In every battery cycle (charging and discharging), optimiser runs for every charging cycle. Once the optimal input is found, it simulates the charging, following the constant current discharging. Amplitude stress can be estimated in each cycle by the stresses generated in charging and discharging. After discharging, voltage check is performed so that it maintained before every cycle. Non-linear model predictive control (NMPC) strategy is implemented considering initial state of charge (SOC) as 0.4 while current while maximum current upper bounds varies from 0.5 C to 4 C in this work. Reference state of charge (
S O
C
r e f
) is taken as 0.90, and voltage must not exceed 4.2 V. Problem ((45)) starts at initial time (
t
0
= 0) and final time (
t
f
) is when state of charge of battery reaches the given reference value. The solver predicts the future instances with a prediction horizon of 100 s using four collocation points at each time step. The solution of current time step is the initial guess of next time instant. After tuning control parameter at different values and varying constraints levels, we come up with a general optimal charging profile which can successfully minimise the damage to the battery. Post processing calculations are done on every profile but the proposed profile gives the best results which will be discussed further.

Fig. 2 shows the benchmark Constant current constant voltage (CCCV) and proposed optimal charging profiles. In CCCV strategy, constant current is applied to the battery until the voltage limit is reached; afterwards it runs on constant voltage in which current keeps on decreasing to lower limit. In proposed strategy, charging is carried out at maximum current on certain time and then it keeps decreasing until it reaches at critical current level (
U
c r
). As
σ
a m p l
is the key variable while analysing cyclic life, it means maximum and minimum stresses need to be evaluated. Studies shows that maximum stress will be reach around 50% of state of charge while minimum stress is normally found when battery is full. Proposed strategy tries to keep the current down to minimise the peak stress while limiting the higher value of charging to keep the stress amplitude lower.
t
d
and
t
s
are the time instances defined as decrement and settling of current respectively. Table 1 tabulates charging times,
U
c r
,
t
d
and
t
s
. Critical current decreases as maximum current upper bound increases. It is due to the reason that charging time in higher current rate tends to decrease. As battery upper bound increases,
t
d
decreases because battery charges more rapidly.
t
s
tends to increase with increasing current upper bound. Optimal and CCCV charging times are also shown in Table 1.

As stress amplitude
σ
a m p l
is the key variable causing fracture in electrode of lithium battery so it is necessary to investigate its effect on charging. Fig. 3 shows the relationship between
σ
a m p l
and maximum current upper bounds after 50 cycles of charging–discharging. The stress amplitude difference between CCCV and optimal profiles is inevitable with minimum percentage difference of 2.5% at 0.5 C while 8.9% is recorded at 4 C. Fig. 4 shows the relationship between stress amplitude and different depth of discharge (DOD) levels at various charging rates after 500 cycles. The initial
S O C
is defined as
90 − D O D
. It is already proven that as current rate increases,
σ
a m p l
increases. But what happens when DOD levels vary? According to earlier research works investigation, as the DOD level increases,
σ
a m p l
also increases. Proposed optimal profile successfully decrease the
σ
a m p l
at every DOD level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison proposed strategy with experimental scenario
ce:section.ce:para:
The proposed NMPC framework results have been compared to those of optimal CCCV using experimental scenario presented in [23]. In that experimental research, it was assumed that SEI break/repair effect is the main source of loss of lithium ions. Fig. 5(a) shows the total capacity loss versus DOD for the optimal CCCV and proposed optimal scenarios. CCCV results shows an exponential increase with the DOD at 50% mean SOC. There is trivial difference as far as low DOD levels concerned. However percentage difference of 11% is recorded in case of 100% DOD. In order to validate the proposed strategy, simulations have been carried out at exactly same conditions as the experiments were performed. Fig. 5(b) shows the maximum tangential stress in the SEI at 100% DOD during a cycle of charging/discharging. In this simulation, 50%
S O
C
m e a n
has been maintained with varying DOD. Detail of
S O
C
m e a n
is given in Table 2. It can be noted from Fig. 5(b) that maximum stress in optimal scenario is slightly less than the optimal CCCV case. At any point of cycle, maximum stress in optimal case is lower than that of CCCV results. Relationship between stress amplitude and depth of discharge is shown in Fig. 5(c). There is no difference recorded at 5, 10, 20 and 50% DOD. The maximum difference is recorded at 100% DOD, which is 6.9 MPa and 7.4 MPa in proposed optimal and experimental scenarios respectively, a percentage difference of 7%. At higher DOD, battery cycles show larger stress oscillations, led to bigger SEI damage. Fig. 5(d) shows the relationship between stress amplitude and depth of discharge at different mean SOCs (20% DOD) for both proposed and CCCV scenarios. There is no difference between proposed and CCCV optimal scenarios as far as 50% mean SOC is concerned. The higher difference between simulations is noted at 10 and 90% mean SOC. It is understandable because at 50%
S O
C
m e a n
, graphite expansion is minimum . It causes less SEI damage than 10% and 90%
S O
C
m e a n
values.

The influence of depth of discharge on ageing of battery material is compared in experimental and optimal scenarios. Simulations are done for 750 cycles, at 50%
S O
C
m e a n
and average voltage of 3.7 V (Fig. 6). Experimental data shows that ageing in a battery is in linear relationship with DOD [23]. Proposed optimal methodology tries to minimise the degradation effect but there is no significant difference recorded at 5, 10 and 20% DOD. Optimal result show some improvements of 2.3%, 10.4% percentage difference at 50 and 100% DOD respectively.

Non-linear model predictive control strategy along with incorporation of mechanical degradation effects is presented in the paper. The proposed methodology is based on the assumption that active material of electrode is not the weakest material but it is SEI layer which break and repair during charging. It is proved that although CCCV is most popular method to charge the battery but it increases the amplitude stresses as compared to proposed methodology. Optimal charging profile is always preferable than CCCV over the range of DOD levels. The proposed NMPC framework is also compared with optimal CCCV using already published experimental scenario. Results confirmed that proposed strategy outperforms optimal CCCV almost at every level. At low DOD levels there are not much difference recorded but efficiency of proposed strategy is quite enormous at 50% and 100% depth of discharge levels.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2023.108050.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary material related to this article.
MMC S1
Supplementary material describes the SEI layer’s analytical model and mathematical detail of problem formulation used in the main article.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Critical review on the degradation mechanisms and recent progress of ni-rich layered oxide cathodes for lithium-ion batteries
EnergyChem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Qingmeng Gan, Ning Qin, Huimin Yuan, Li Lu, Zhenghe Xu, and Zhouguang Lu. Critical review on the degradation mechanisms and recent progress of ni-rich layered oxide cathodes for lithium-ion batteries. EnergyChem, page 100103, 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Choi model-free reconstruction of capacity degradation trajectory of lithium-ion batteries using early cycle data
ETransportation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Seongyoon Kim, Hangsoon Jung, Minho Lee, Yun Young Choi, and Jung-Il Choi. Model-free reconstruction of capacity degradation trajectory of lithium-ion batteries using early cycle data. eTransportation, page 100243, 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optimal configuration and operation for user-side energy storage considering lithium-ion battery degradation
Int. J. Electr. Power Energy Syst.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zheng Chen, Zhenyu Li, and Guozhu Chen. Optimal configuration and operation for user-side energy storage considering lithium-ion battery degradation. International Journal of Electrical Power & Energy Systems, 145:108621, 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium-ion battery remaining useful life prediction using a two-phase degradation model with a dynamic change point
J. Energy Storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Rui Wang, Mengmeng Zhu, Xiangwu Zhang, and Hoang Pham. Lithium-ion battery remaining useful life prediction using a two-phase degradation model with a dynamic change point. Journal of Energy Storage, 59:106457, 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Calendar aging of a graphite/lifepo4 cell
J. Power Sources

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mohammad Kassem, Julien Bernard, Renaud Revel, Serge Pelissier, François Duclaud, and C Delacourt. Calendar aging of a graphite/lifepo4 cell. Journal of Power Sources, 208:296–305, 2012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Calendar and phev cycle life aging of high-energy, lithium-ion cells containing blended spinel and layered-oxide cathodes
J. Power Sources

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J Belt, V Utgikar, and I Bloom. Calendar and phev cycle life aging of high-energy, lithium-ion cells containing blended spinel and layered-oxide cathodes. Journal of Power Sources, 196(23):10213–10221, 2011.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solvent diffusion model for aging of lithium-ion battery cells
J. Electrochem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Harry J Ploehn, Premanand Ramadass, and Ralph E White. Solvent diffusion model for aging of lithium-ion battery cells. Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 151(3):A456–A462, 2004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Calendar and cycle life study of li (nimnco) o2-based 18650 lithium-ion batteries
J. Power Sources

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Madeleine Ecker, Nerea Nieto, Stefan Käbitz, Johannes Schmalstieg, Holger Blanke, Alexander Warnecke, and Dirk Uwe Sauer. Calendar and cycle life study of li (nimnco) o2-based 18650 lithium-ion batteries. Journal of Power Sources, 248:839–851, 2014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of porous structure and morphology of cathode on the degradation of lithium-ion batteries
J. Energy Storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yoon Koo Lee. Effect of porous structure and morphology of cathode on the degradation of lithium-ion batteries. Journal of Energy Storage, 52:104788, 2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigation on lithium-ion battery degradation induced by combined effect of current rate and operating temperature during fast charging
J. Energy Storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: JG Qu, ZY Jiang, and JF Zhang. Investigation on lithium-ion battery degradation induced by combined effect of current rate and operating temperature during fast charging. Journal of Energy Storage, 52:104811, 2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stress generation and fracture in lithium insertion materials
J. Solid State Electrochem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: John Christensen and John Newman. Stress generation and fracture in lithium insertion materials. Journal of Solid State Electrochemistry, 10(5):293–319, 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A mathematical model of stress generation and fracture in lithium manganese oxide
J. Electrochem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: John Christensen and John Newman. A mathematical model of stress generation and fracture in lithium manganese oxide. Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 153(6):A1019–A1030, 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Numerical simulation of intercalation-induced stress in li-ion battery electrode particles
J. Electrochem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xiangchun Zhang, Wei Shyy, and Ann Marie Sastry. Numerical simulation of intercalation-induced stress in li-ion battery electrode particles. Journal of the Electrochemical Society, 154(10):A910–A916, 2007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Numerical simulation of stress evolution in lithium manganese dioxide particles due to coupled phase transition and intercalation
J. Electrochem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jonghyun Park, Wei Lu, and Ann Marie Sastry. Numerical simulation of stress evolution in lithium manganese dioxide particles due to coupled phase transition and intercalation. Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 158(2):A201–A206, 2011.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Threefold increase in the young’s modulus of graphite negative electrode during lithium intercalation
J. Electrochem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yue Qi, Haibo Guo, Louis G Hector, and Adam Timmons. Threefold increase in the young’s modulus of graphite negative electrode during lithium intercalation. Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 157(5):A558–A566, 2010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Battery cycle life prediction with coupled chemical degradation and fatigue mechanics
J. Electrochem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Rutooj Deshpande, Mark Verbrugge, Yang-Tse Cheng, John Wang, and Ping Liu. Battery cycle life prediction with coupled chemical degradation and fatigue mechanics. Journal of the Electrochemical Society, 159(10):A1730–A1738, 2012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Degradation of lithium ion batteries employing graphite negatives and nickel–cobalt–manganese oxide+ spinel manganese oxide positives: Part 1, aging mechanisms and life estimation
J. Power Sources

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: John Wang, Justin Purewal, Ping Liu, Jocelyn Hicks-Garner, Souren Soukazian, Elena Sherman, Adam Sorenson, Luan Vu, Harshad Tataria, and Mark W Verbrugge. Degradation of lithium ion batteries employing graphite negatives and nickel–cobalt–manganese oxide+ spinel manganese oxide positives: Part 1, aging mechanisms and life estimation. Journal of Power Sources, 269:937–948, 2014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Examination of graphite particle cracking as a failure mode in lithium-ion batteries: a model-experimental study
J. Electrochem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kenji Takahashi and Venkat Srinivasan. Examination of graphite particle cracking as a failure mode in lithium-ion batteries: a model-experimental study. Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 162(4):A635–A645, 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of the features and analyses of the solid electrolyte interphase in li-ion batteries
Electrochim. Acta
